color_profiles.name: blue-moonstone
colors: colorless to white body; blue floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Thin albite exsolution lamellae (10–100 nm) produce Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive interference for blue wavelengths.

color_profiles.name: white-moonstone
colors: white body; white to milky-white floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Thicker lamellae scatter all visible wavelengths approximately equally, producing white adularescence.

color_profiles.name: peach-moonstone
colors: warm-beige body; peach to white glow
color_profiles.cause: Iron impurities impart warm body color; adularescence still present.

color_profiles.name: rainbow-moonstone
colors: colorless to white body; multicolor iridescence — blue
color_profiles.cause: Trade name for colorless to white labradorite showing adularescence-like labradorescence. Not orthoclase moonstone.

color_profiles.name: gray-moonstone
colors: gray body; blue to white floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Iron or other inclusions giving gray body color; adularescence retained.

color_profiles.name: cat-eye-moonstone
color_profiles.cause: Needle-like inclusions aligned with exsolution lamellae create chatoyancy in addition to adularescence.

visual_notes: Adularescence appears as a floating, glowing light phenomenon that rolls or shifts as the stone is moved — unlike surface iridescence, it appears to emanate from within the stone. The finest blue moonstone from Sri Lanka has an electric, almost liquid blue light that rolls across the interior.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak bluish or yellowish fluorescence in some specimens under longwave UV.
optical_effects.iridescence: Adularescence: the defining optical phenomenon. A floating, billowing blue or white light glow from alternating orthoclase/albite exsolution lamellae. Caused by Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive thin-film interference. Blue adularescence (from thin lamellae) is most prized. Effect is direction-dependent and orientation-critical in cutting.
optical_effects.play_of_color: Adularescence: the defining optical phenomenon. A floating, billowing blue or white light glow from alternating orthoclase/albite exsolution lamellae. Caused by Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive thin-film interference. Blue adularescence (from thin lamellae) is most prized. Effect is direction-dependent and orientation-critical in cutting.
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; cat's eye moonstone exists with needle inclusions.
